content.text: Launch Readiness Tasks and Owner Assignments

A launch checklist without named owners is a wish list. When every task has a team and a person attached to it, accountability is clear, status is trackable, and the Go/No-Go meeting becomes a brief status read rather than an hour of ambiguity.
The checklist is not a document to fill in the day before launch. It is a living coordination artifact that each owning team updates as their tasks complete across the two-week pre-launch window.

This sub-subtopic covers the complete owner-assigned task structure across all functions, the cadence for tracking progress, what a Go/No-Go meeting actually decides, and how to handle last-minute blockers without derailing the launch date.

attrs.alt: Launch readiness swimlane diagram showing tasks for Engineering, SRE, Security, AI Safety, Legal, Finance, and Comms teams from T-14 to launch day

content.text: The following table covers the canonical set of launch tasks. Each row names the owning function, the task, the evidence that closes the task, and the latest it can be completed without blocking launch.
Open PRR doc and complete self-assessed checklist
PRR document shared with all reviewers in the project wiki
Open exception YAMLs for any unmet criteria
Exception files in docs/prr/exceptions/, tagged to domain reviewer
Load test at 2× expected peak RPS
Load test report attached to PRR; no error spike or circuit breaker trip
Resolve all P0 PRR open items
Each P0 row in PRR scorecard shows APPROVED status
Canary deploy confirmed; monitoring dashboard link in launch doc
Validate rollback from canary to production version
Rollback executed and re-confirmed in staging during drill
Version endpoint shows production version on all instances
Review SLO definition and error budget policy
SLO document approved; alert thresholds set in Prometheus/alertmanager
Confirm all alert rules are live and tested
Synthetic error injected; PagerDuty alert fires within 5 min
Drill log entry with timestamp and elapsed time < 10 min
SRE approval comment in PRR scorecard
Grype container scan: zero unmitigated CRITICAL CVEs
Grype report attached to release artifact; CRITICAL count = 0 or exceptions filed
CI run link showing pip-audit pass on the production build
S3 URI of CycloneDX SBOM for the production build version
Rotation date in secrets manager metadata
Security approval comment in PRR scorecard
Red-team results reviewed; no unmitigated P0/P1 findings
Red-team report link; all P0/P1 findings marked mitigated or exception filed
Output filter live and tested in staging
Filter test results showing refusal rate on adversarial inputs
AST-based scan script output with zero system-prompt injection risks
AI Safety approval comment in PRR scorecard
Data flow diagram reviewed and approved
Diagram showing all PII fields and their API usage documented
User consent mechanism live (if user PII sent to API)
Consent UI screenshot or API contract confirming consent gate
Data retention policy documented and implemented
Retention schedule in runbook; log truncation job confirmed active
Legal approval comment in PRR scorecard
Monthly token budget alert configured in Anthropic Console
Console screenshot showing alert at 80% and 100% of budget
Cost projection for peak load approved
Cost model spreadsheet reviewed and within approved budget envelope
Per-request cost tracking in production DB confirmed
DB schema or query showing anthropic_input_tokens and output_tokens per request
Status page entry prepared (not yet published)
Status page draft reviewed; ready to publish within 5 min of any incident
Blog post / release note draft reviewed and approved
Tracking Status in the Launch Document
The launch document is updated daily by each owning team. A simple status column keeps the cross-functional picture current without requiring a daily meeting:

# docs/launches/triage-api-v2.1.0.yaml
service: triage-api
version: "2.1.0"
launch_date: "2026-07-22"
launch_owner: alice@example.com    # engineering lead; accountable for overall status

tasks:
  - id: prr-doc-open
    owner_function: Engineering
    owner: alice@example.com
    title: Open PRR doc and complete self-assessed checklist
    due: T-14
    status: DONE
    evidence: https://wiki.example.com/prr/triage-api-v2.1.0

  - id: load-test
    owner_function: Engineering
    owner: alice@example.com
    title: Load test at 2x peak RPS
    due: T-10
    status: DONE
    evidence: https://github.com/org/triage-api/actions/runs/654321

  - id: grype-scan
    owner_function: Security
    owner: bob.jones@example.com
    title: Grype container scan — zero unmitigated CRITICAL CVEs
    due: T-10
    status: DONE
    evidence: s3://acme-builds/triage-api/v2.1.0/grype-report.json

  - id: red-team
    owner_function: AI Safety
    owner: carol@example.com
    title: Red-team results reviewed; no unmitigated P0/P1 findings
    due: T-12
    status: IN_PROGRESS
    evidence: null
    notes: "Red-team session scheduled 2026-07-16"

  - id: budget-alert
    owner_function: Finance
    owner: eve@example.com
    title: Monthly token budget alert configured
    due: T-12
    status: DONE
    evidence: https://console.anthropic.com/alerts (screenshot attached)
The launch owner runs a 10-minute async status update at the start of each day from T-7 onward, tagging any task that has gone red with the blocking owner.

The Go/No-Go meeting happens at T-0 morning, typically 30 minutes. Its purpose is not to discuss — all discussions should have happened during the PRR review window. It is a status read and a binary decision.

Go/No-Go Meeting — Triage API v2.1.0 — 2026-07-22 09:00
Attendees: launch owner + one representative per owning function

Agenda (30 min):
1. Launch owner reads the status of every OPEN task (5 min)
2. Each function representative confirms their task status:
   - SRE: "Alert rules live, rollback drill done in 8 min — GREEN"
   - Security: "SBOM uploaded, Grype clear, API key rotated — GREEN"
   - AI Safety: "Output filter live, no unmitigated findings — GREEN"
   - Legal: "Data flow approved, consent live — GREEN"
   - Finance: "Budget alert set, cost projection within envelope — GREEN"
3. Any representative may call RED — launches with any RED are BLOCKED
4. Launch owner calls GO or NO-GO (2 min)
5. If GO: canary monitoring period confirmed; rollout starts at [time]
6. If NO-GO: blocking item named, owner named, re-assessment time set

Decision recorded in the launch document with timestamp.

A Go/No-Go should never last more than 30 minutes. If the meeting runs long, it is a sign that tasks were not tracked during the pre-launch window and discussions that should have happened at T-7 are happening now.
A blocker discovered at T-0 or T-1 requires a fast decision: fix it in time, accept it as an exception, or delay the launch. The decision tree:
Can the blocker be fixed within 4 hours?
If yes, fix it, update evidence, and call a second Go/No-Go at the new time. Do not launch until the fix is confirmed.
Can the blocker be accepted as a time-bounded exception?
If yes and the domain reviewer agrees, file the exception YAML, get approval (async via Slack is acceptable at T-0), and proceed. The exception must have a ≤ 7-day expiry.
the launch is delayed. Announce the new date within 2 hours. A delayed launch with a clear new date is better than a compromised launch or a rushed exception.
Common last-minute blockers and their typical paths:
Alert rule not firing in test
Grype finds a new CRITICAL CVE in a base image update
Pin base image to previous tag; exception for upgrade
Cap initial traffic at 50% of peak; exception with monitoring plan
Hard block — no exception path; delay launch until consent is live
Budget alert not configured in Console
For teams that store their launch document as YAML in Git, a script can generate a status dashboard from the task file, replacing the need to manually compile a status email:

# scripts/launch_status_report.py
import yaml, sys
from pathlib import Path
from collections import defaultdict

LAUNCH_FILE = Path(sys.argv[1])  # e.g. docs/launches/triage-api-v2.1.0.yaml
doc = yaml.safe_load(LAUNCH_FILE.read_text())

by_function = defaultdict(list)
for task in doc.get("tasks", []):
    by_function[task["owner_function"]].append(task)

print(f"\n=== Launch Status: {doc['service']} v{doc['version']} ===")
print(f"Launch date: {doc['launch_date']}  Owner: {doc['launch_owner']}\n")

overall_go = True
for function, tasks in sorted(by_function.items()):
    done    = [t for t in tasks if t["status"] == "DONE"]
    in_prog = [t for t in tasks if t["status"] == "IN_PROGRESS"]
    not_started = [t for t in tasks if t["status"] not in ("DONE", "IN_PROGRESS")]
    fn_color = "GREEN" if not in_prog and not not_started else "YELLOW" if not not_started else "RED"
    if fn_color == "RED": overall_go = False

    print(f"  [{fn_color:6}] {function}: {len(done)}/{len(tasks)} done")
    for t in in_prog:
        print(f"           IN_PROGRESS: {t['title']} ({t['owner']})")
    for t in not_started:
        print(f"           BLOCKED:     {t['title']} ({t['owner']})")

print(f"\nOverall: {'GO' if overall_go else 'NO-GO'}")
The launch is not complete at T-0 rollout. The monitoring window is a defined period during which the engineering and SRE owners watch key metrics and are prepared to roll back without discussion:
Error rate, refusal rate, latency p99, token usage
Error rate > 1% OR latency p99 > 5s sustained for 10 min
Error rate > 0.5% OR cost > 2× projection for 1 hour

The monitoring window owner is named in the launch document and is distinct from the on-call engineer. Their role is to watch the dashboards and make the rollback call without waiting for an alert — proactive, not reactive.

Several tasks on the launch checklist can be verified by script rather than by a person. Running these checks as part of the CD pipeline removes the risk of human error and speeds up the Go/No-Go meeting preparation.

#!/bin/bash
# scripts/run_launch_gates.sh
# Runs all automated launch-readiness checks for a given service and version.
# Call from CD pipeline at T-0, before the Go/No-Go meeting.
set -euo pipefail
SERVICE="$1"   # e.g. triage-api
VERSION="$2"   # e.g. 2.1.0
FAILURES=0

check() {
  local name="$1"; shift
  if "$@" > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    echo "PASS: $name"
  else
    echo "FAIL: $name"
    FAILURES=$((FAILURES+1))
  fi
}

# Engineering gates
check "prompt pins present in production config" \
  python3 scripts/check_prompt_pins.py

check "no API key in source" \
  bash -c '! grep -rq "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY\s*=" src/'

check "all claude calls have explicit timeout" \
  python3 scripts/check_claude_timeouts.py

# Security gates
check "Grype scan passed (no CRITICAL)" \
  bash -c 'jq ".matches | map(select(.vulnerability.severity=="Critical")) | length == 0" grype-report.json'

check "SBOM exists in registry" \
  aws s3 ls "s3://acme-builds/${SERVICE}/${VERSION}/sbom.json"

# PRR gate
check "PRR scorecard all-approved" \
  python3 scripts/check_prr_gate.py "$SERVICE" "$VERSION"

# Exception gate
check "no expired exceptions" \
  python3 scripts/check_exceptions_for_deploy.py "$SERVICE" "$VERSION"

# Observability gate
check "prometheus metrics endpoint live" \
  curl -sf "http://localhost:8000/metrics" | grep -q "prompt_requests_total"

echo ""
if [ "$FAILURES" -gt 0 ]; then
  echo "$FAILURES launch gate(s) failed — NO-GO."
  exit 1
fi
echo "All launch gates passed — ready for Go/No-Go meeting."

Each failed gate produces a named output line. The Go/No-Go meeting starts with the script's output on screen — the launch owner reads the FAILs, each owning function resolves theirs, and the meeting closes when the script passes clean.

The checklist evolves as the team learns from incidents. Significant changes to required tasks or owner assignments should be versioned so that ongoing launches are not disrupted mid-window by a checklist update:

# docs/launches/_checklist-version.yaml
version: "3.2"
effective_date: "2026-07-01"
changes_from_3_1:
  - Added: AI Safety sign-off as P0 requirement (was P1)
  - Added: Cost projection approval by Finance (new task)
  - Changed: Load test threshold from 1.5× to 2× peak RPS
  - Removed: Manual SBOM upload (now automated in CI — PR #455)
applies_to_launches_after: "2026-07-01"

# scripts/validate_launch_doc_version.py
import yaml, sys
from pathlib import Path

CURRENT_CHECKLIST_VERSION = "3.2"

launch_doc = yaml.safe_load(Path(sys.argv[1]).read_text())
doc_version = launch_doc.get("checklist_version", "unknown")

if doc_version != CURRENT_CHECKLIST_VERSION:
    print(
        f"WARN: Launch document uses checklist v{doc_version}; "
        f"current is v{CURRENT_CHECKLIST_VERSION}. "
        f"Review the changelog and update the task list if needed."
    )
    sys.exit(1)

print(f"Checklist version OK: {doc_version}")

Launches that started their window before a checklist update are not required to retrofit the new tasks if they are beyond T-7. Add the changed tasks to their exception list instead, and apply them from the next launch cycle.
Common Failure Patterns in Launch Execution
Tasks assigned to a function, not a person
No one feels individually accountable; tasks slip
Every task row names one person with an email address
PRR opened at T-3 instead of T-14
Reviewers have no time; blockers found at T-0
PRR doc mandatory at T-14; launch owner tracks SLA
Discussions that should have happened at T-7 happen now
PRR review window resolves all debates; Go/No-Go is a read, not a discussion
No one is watching the dashboard after rollout
Name the monitoring window owner in the launch doc before T-0
Team debates whether to roll back while users are affected
Define numeric rollback triggers (error rate %, latency threshold) in launch doc
Post-launch tasks never tracked to closure
Open items drift; next launch inherits the debt
Post-launch section in launch doc with owner and due date; closure required before next launch
every task on the launch checklist names the owning function and a specific person; "Engineering" is not an owner.
all tasks carry a 'latest by' deadline referenced to the launch date; the furthest back are T-14 (PRR doc, data flow review, budget alert).
the launch owner runs a 10-minute async status update each morning; blocking tasks are escalated immediately, not at the Go/No-Go meeting.
30 minutes, each function reports GREEN or RED, any RED blocks the launch, decision is recorded with timestamp.
4-hour fix or exception path or launch delay — no in-between; consent and legal blockers cannot be exception-pathed.
YAML-based launch document enables a script to generate status reports without manual aggregation.
canary (24h) → 25% ramp (6h) → 100% with 48h watch; named monitoring owner separate from on-call, with pre-agreed rollback triggers.
